title: Semiclassical Ehrenfest paths in open quantum systems
abstract: We study the semiclassical Ehrenfest trajectories in open quantum systems. We first derive in explicit form the Fokker-Planck equation that governs the time evolution of the mixing measure for a Gaussian mixture. Then, we embed the generalized Ehrenfest theorem recently obtained for open quantum systems into this phase-space picture to study the time evolution of the expectations of observable with respect to the Gaussian mixture. We show how the coherent and irreversible contributions are microscopically separated. Our work provides a transparent phase-space interpretation of the emergence of classical trajectories in open quantum dynamics.
summary: This paper develops a phase-space framework to describe semiclassical Ehrenfest trajectories in open quantum systems using a Gaussian mixture representation. It successfully demonstrates how the evolution of quantum expectation values can be split into coherent motion and irreversible diffusion. This provides a clear physical picture of how environmental effects like dissipation and decoherence lead to the emergence of classical-like trajectories.
main_problem: Extending semiclassical Ehrenfest trajectories from closed to open quantum systems and providing a phase-space interpretation of how classical trajectories emerge from open quantum dynamics.
main_result: The derivation of a Fokker-Planck equation for a Gaussian mixture representation that allows for the microscopic separation of coherent and irreversible (diffusive) contributions to the evolution of expectation values.
method: The study uses a Gaussian mixture representation of the density matrix, a Local Harmonic Approximation, and a Lagrangian description via a Fokker-Planck equation, validated by Monte-Carlo simulations using Ito stochastic differential equations.
model_or_system: Open quantum systems governed by the Lindblad master equation, with a specific validation model consisting of a 1D free particle subject to momentum diffusion via a Lindblad operator.
key_observables: Expectation values of observables (via Weyl symbols), canonical operators (position and momentum), position variance, and thermodynamic quantities like reversible work and heat.
important_parameters: Centroid (position and momentum), covariance matrix, diffusion matrix, friction vector, coupling strength (lambda), and the 'not-too-squeezed' (NTS) condition.
main_assumptions: The Local Harmonic Approximation is used, which assumes the dynamics can be truncated to second order in Weyl symbols and that Gaussian states do not become excessively squeezed.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares the evolution of position variance over time for the quantum case (showing growth from dispersion and diffusion), a classical comparison, and the exact analytical solution.
paper_structure: The paper first derives the Fokker-Planck equation for the mixing measure of a Gaussian mixture, then embeds a generalized Ehrenfest theorem into this phase-space picture to separate coherent and irreversible dynamics, and finally validates the framework using a 1D free particle model with momentum diffusion.
why_interesting: It provides a transparent microscopic mechanism for the emergence of classicality from open quantum dynamics, specifically by decomposing the evolution of observables into unitary and dissipative components, which is central to understanding decoherence.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The position variance ⟨ˆx2⟩(t) = x(t)2 + σxx(t) for three cases: The quantum case with Dab = diag(2ℏλ, 0) (blue dotted line); the classical case with D = 0 (grey dotted line); the analytical quantum solution (red line). The choices of parameters are specified in the main text.
